sources: Pavilion / Revenue Collective community case studies
SaaStr Annual session data and founder interviews
Synthesized composites from recurring B2B SaaS failure/success modes
meta.note: All archetypes are anonymized composites. No single archetype represents a specific company.

archetypes.name: The Founder Bottleneck

constraintPattern.description: Founder closes 80%+ of deals, no documented sales process, team can't sell without founder in the room

sequence: Documented founder's implicit sales playbook (week 1-2)
Hired first AE with founder shadowing period (week 3-6)
Built basic objection-handling library from recorded calls (week 4-8)
Transitioned founder to strategic/enterprise only (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: Recording and transcribing every founder sales call for 2 weeks to extract the implicit playbook

outcome.days90: AE closing 40% of pipeline independently. Founder time on sales dropped from 70% to 25%.
outcome.months6: MRR grew 85% (from ~€15k to ~€28k). Second AE hired. Founder focused on product and partnerships.

archetypes.keyLesson: Most founders resist documenting their sales process because they believe it's intuitive. The breakthrough comes from recording, not interviewing — founders don't know what they do differently until they hear themselves do it.

archetypes.name: The Leaky Bucket

constraintPattern.description: Monthly churn >6%, aggressive acquisition spending but no retention infrastructure, onboarding is a PDF and a welcome email

sequence: Exit interview blitz — called every churned customer from last 6 months (week 1-2)
Rebuilt onboarding into 14-day guided activation sequence (week 2-5)
Introduced health score model with 3 leading indicators (week 4-7)
Shifted 30% of acquisition budget to customer success (week 6-12)
intervention.keyAction: The exit interview blitz revealed that 60% of churn happened before day 30 — an onboarding problem disguised as a product problem

outcome.days90: Monthly churn dropped from 7.2% to 3.8%. Saved ~€7k/mo in retained revenue.
outcome.months6: Churn stabilized at 2.5%. NRR improved from 85% to 105%. CAC payback dropped from 18 to 11 months.

archetypes.keyLesson: Companies with high churn instinctively try to out-acquire the problem. The counterintuitive move is to freeze acquisition spending and redirect it to retention — the math always favors saving a customer over acquiring a new one at 5x the cost.

archetypes.name: The Premature Scaler

constraintPattern.description: Hired 3+ salespeople before proving repeatable sales motion, CAC is 3x stage median, win rate <15%, pipeline is large but conversion is terrible

sequence: Paused all outbound hiring, reduced team to top 2 performers (week 1-2)
Founder re-entered sales to revalidate ICP and messaging (week 1-4)
Ran 30-day ICP tightening sprint — analyzed all won deals for pattern (week 2-6)
Rebuilt sales playbook around narrow ICP before re-scaling (week 6-12)
intervention.keyAction: Shrinking the team was emotionally painful but freed cash to survive the ICP pivot. The top 2 AEs outperformed the original team of 5.

outcome.days90: Win rate improved from 12% to 28%. CAC dropped 55%. Pipeline shrank 40% but revenue stayed flat.
outcome.months6: MRR doubled. Hired 2 new AEs with proven playbook. CAC payback dropped from 22 to 9 months.

archetypes.keyLesson: A shrinking pipeline with a rising win rate is a sign of health, not decline. Most seed-stage companies over-index on pipeline volume when they should be optimizing for pipeline quality. Fewer, better opportunities always beat more, worse ones.

archetypes.name: The Invisible Funnel

constraintPattern.description: No CRM or CRM is a graveyard, founder 'knows' the pipeline in their head, can't answer how many deals are in each stage, forecasting is gut-feel

sequence: Implemented lightweight CRM with 4 pipeline stages (week 1-2)
Backfilled current pipeline from founder's memory + email (week 2-3)
Established weekly 15-min pipeline review ritual (week 3-4)
Built simple dashboard: deals by stage, velocity, and aging (week 4-8)
intervention.keyAction: The backfill exercise revealed 8 'dead' deals the founder still counted as pipeline — removing them gave an honest baseline

outcome.days90: Pipeline coverage went from 'unknown' to 3.2x. Identified 3 stuck deals worth €45k collectively. Closed 2 of them.
outcome.months6: Forecast accuracy reached 70% (from ~30% gut-feel). Sales cycle shortened 20% due to proactive deal management.

archetypes.keyLesson: Pipeline visibility isn't about the tool — it's about the ritual. A spreadsheet reviewed weekly beats Salesforce ignored daily. The 15-minute weekly review is the single highest-ROI habit a seed-stage founder can adopt.

archetypes.name: The Pricing Paralysis

constraintPattern.description: ACV hasn't changed in 18+ months, pricing is based on competitor benchmarking not value delivered, no expansion revenue path, all customers pay roughly the same

sequence: Willingness-to-pay survey to top 20 customers (week 1-3)
Identified value metric and restructured pricing tiers (week 3-6)
Tested new pricing on new deals only — kept existing customers on old plan (week 6-10)
Introduced annual plans with 15% discount to improve cash flow (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: The willingness-to-pay survey showed 65% of customers would have paid 40-60% more — the founder had been anchoring to competitor pricing instead of value delivered

outcome.days90: Average new deal size increased 45%. First expansion deal closed at 2x original ACV.
outcome.months6: ARPA up 60%. NRR improved from 95% to 115% via natural tier upgrades. No increase in churn from repricing.

archetypes.keyLesson: Founders systematically underprice by 30-50% because they fear churn from price increases. In practice, well-communicated value-based repricing almost never increases churn — and the customers who leave on price were never your ICP.

archetypes.name: The Channel Scatter

constraintPattern.description: Running 5+ channels simultaneously with no clear winner, can't attribute revenue to source, marketing budget spread thin across channels, no channel has reached escape velocity

sequence: Attribution audit — mapped last 20 closed-won deals to original source (week 1-3)
Identified top 2 channels by actual closed-won attribution (week 3-4)
Killed underperforming channels, doubled down on top 2 (week 4-6)
Built measurement framework to track channel-level CAC and payback (week 6-12)
intervention.keyAction: The attribution audit showed 70% of revenue came from just 2 of the 6 active channels — the other 4 were consuming 50% of the budget

outcome.days90: Marketing-sourced pipeline increased 35% while budget decreased 20%. CAC dropped 40%.
outcome.months6: Primary channel reached 3x original volume. Second channel generated first partner referrals. Overall CAC payback dropped from 16 to 10 months.

archetypes.keyLesson: Seed-stage companies should own 1-2 channels, not rent 6. The temptation to 'test everything' leads to data poverty on every channel. You need 30+ data points per channel to draw conclusions — most seed companies don't have enough pipeline to feed 3+.

archetypes.name: The Product-Led Plateau

constraintPattern.description: Strong free/trial signups but <3% conversion to paid, no sales-assist layer, product usage data exists but isn't used for outreach, enterprise prospects self-serve and get stuck

sequence: Built PQL scoring model from usage data — 5 activation signals (week 1-4)
Added sales-assist layer: AE reaches out when PQL score triggers (week 4-6)
Introduced reverse trial — full features for 14 days then downgrade (week 6-8)
Built enterprise-specific onboarding path with white-glove option (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: The PQL model revealed that users who completed 3 specific actions in the first 7 days converted at 8x the rate — this became the activation checklist

outcome.days90: Trial-to-paid conversion improved from 2.8% to 6.5%. First enterprise deal closed via sales-assist at 4x average ACV.
outcome.months6: Hybrid PLG+sales motion established. Enterprise segment contributing 35% of new MRR. Overall conversion at 7.2%.

archetypes.keyLesson: Pure PLG hits a ceiling around €50-80k MRR unless you add a sales-assist layer. The mistake is treating PLG and sales-led as opposites — the winners use product data to tell sales exactly when and who to call.

archetypes.name: The Delivery Trap

constraintPattern.description: Founder spends 80%+ time on delivery, no time for sales, revenue is capped by founder's billable hours, every client needs the founder personally

sequence: Productized the most common 70% of delivery into a repeatable framework (week 1-4)
Hired a junior delivery person to handle productized work (week 4-6)
Founder shifted to sales 3 days/week, delivery oversight 2 days/week (week 6-8)
Introduced group delivery model — serve 3 clients with shared sessions (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: Productizing delivery meant accepting that 70% of client work was actually the same across clients — the founder's belief that 'every client is unique' was the bottleneck

outcome.days90: Client capacity increased from 3 to 7 without quality drop. Founder selling 3 days/week generated 4 new qualified opportunities.
outcome.months6: MRR grew from €8k to €22k. Founder down to 1 day/week delivery. Hired second delivery person.

archetypes.keyLesson: Service founders build a job, not a business, until they separate delivery from their personal time. The breakthrough is accepting that 80% standardized delivery is better for the client AND the business than 100% bespoke work.

archetypes.name: The Enterprise Misfit

constraintPattern.description: Sales cycles >90 days, targeting enterprise buyers with seed-stage resources, cash burn is front-loaded but revenue is delayed, pipeline looks big but nothing closes

sequence: Segmented pipeline: created a mid-market tier with shorter cycle (week 1-3)
Built 'quick win' package — limited scope, faster deployment, proof of concept (week 3-6)
Introduced land-and-expand model: start small, grow inside account (week 6-8)
Shifted 50% of outbound effort to mid-market while nurturing enterprise long-term (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: Creating a 'starter' package at 30% of enterprise ACV shortened the cycle from 90+ days to 25 days — then 40% of starters expanded to full within 6 months

outcome.days90: Average sales cycle dropped from 95 to 35 days for new segment. Pipeline coverage went from 1.5x to 4x.
outcome.months6: Mid-market contributing 55% of new MRR. First 3 enterprise expansions closed. Cash flow positive for first time.

archetypes.keyLesson: Selling enterprise at seed stage is like fighting gravity — the unit economics only work if you can land small and expand. The companies that succeed go mid-market first to build references, then use those references to open enterprise doors.

archetypes.name: The Solo AE Ceiling

constraintPattern.description: One AE doing everything — prospecting, qualifying, closing, onboarding. Pipeline generation dies when AE is in closing cycles. Feast-famine revenue pattern.

sequence: Split AE's time: mornings for prospecting blocks, afternoons for pipeline (week 1-2)
Built automated outbound sequences to generate baseline pipeline (week 2-5)
Hired part-time SDR to take over top-of-funnel qualification (week 5-8)
Established weekly pipeline generation targets separate from quota (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: Separating prospecting from closing as distinct scheduled activities (not ad hoc) broke the feast-famine cycle within 3 weeks

outcome.days90: Pipeline generation became consistent at 12+ qualified opps/month (was 4-15 swing). AE close rate improved 30% due to focus.
outcome.months6: SDR ramped to full-time. Total pipeline 3x initial. Hired second AE. Revenue predictability enabled first annual plan.

archetypes.keyLesson: The most expensive mistake at seed stage isn't hiring wrong — it's asking your AE to prospect and close simultaneously. These require different mental states and compete for calendar space. Even a part-time SDR (or automated sequences) breaks the cycle.

archetypes.name: The Metrics Desert

constraintPattern.description: Can't answer basic unit economics questions — CAC, LTV, payback, magic number. Scaling decisions made on revenue growth alone. Board sees topline but not efficiency.

sequence: Built SaaS metrics dashboard with 8 core metrics from existing data (week 1-3)
Retroactively calculated CAC, LTV, payback from last 12 months (week 2-4)
Established monthly metrics review cadence with leadership (week 4-6)
Used metrics to kill 2 unprofitable customer segments (week 6-12)
intervention.keyAction: The retroactive CAC analysis showed one channel had 28-month payback while another had 8-month — they were spending equally on both

outcome.days90: Full SaaS metrics dashboard live. Killed unprofitable segment saving €6k/mo. Reallocated budget to high-LTV segments.
outcome.months6: Blended CAC payback improved from 17 to 10 months. Board confidence increased — approved Series A fundraise at higher valuation.

archetypes.keyLesson: Revenue growth without unit economics visibility is running in the dark. Most early-scale companies discover they have 2-3 customer segments blended together — and at least one is destroying margin. The dashboard doesn't just track; it reveals which growth is healthy and which is toxic.

archetypes.name: The Outbound Grind

constraintPattern.description: 100% dependent on outbound, no content or inbound flywheel, growth is linear to effort — every deal requires fresh prospecting, no compounding mechanics

sequence: Launched weekly content from sales insights — turned objections into articles (week 1-4)
Built referral system with structured ask at customer success milestones (week 3-6)
Created partner program with 2 complementary non-competing tools (week 6-10)
Established LinkedIn founder-brand posting cadence — 3x/week from sales learnings (week 2-12)
intervention.keyAction: Turning sales objections into content was zero-cost and solved two problems — inbound pipeline AND better sales enablement. Each objection article became a nurture asset.

outcome.days90: Inbound leads went from 0 to 15% of pipeline. First 2 partner referrals closed. LinkedIn presence generating 3-5 warm DMs/week.
outcome.months6: Inbound at 35% of pipeline. Partner channel at 10%. Outbound still dominant but team has 3 revenue sources instead of 1.

archetypes.keyLesson: Pure outbound is a treadmill — you run faster every month for the same result. The escape is not abandoning outbound but building compounding channels alongside it. Founder-brand content is the highest-ROI seed-stage inbound investment because it costs nothing but time.

archetypes.name: The Config Chaos

constraintPattern.description: 10+ disconnected tools, no single source of truth, data lives in silos, team spends hours on manual reporting, CRM is unreliable because nobody updates it

sequence: Tool audit — mapped every tool to owner, cost, and actual usage (week 1-2)
Eliminated 4 redundant tools, saved €3.2k/mo (week 2-4)
Built core integration: CRM ↔ marketing automation ↔ CS platform (week 4-8)
Automated 3 key reports that previously took 5+ hours/week manual effort (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: The tool audit revealed 3 teams maintaining separate spreadsheets that tracked the same data — unifying into CRM saved 12 hours/week across the team

outcome.days90: Eliminated €3.2k/mo in redundant tools. Saved 15 hours/week in manual reporting. CRM adoption went from 40% to 85%.
outcome.months6: Single source of truth established. Forecasting accuracy improved from 55% to 80%. RevOps function created to maintain systems.

archetypes.keyLesson: Tool sprawl is a symptom, not a cause. The root problem is that each team solved their workflow gap independently. RevOps as a function doesn't exist to manage tools — it exists to design workflows that span teams. Start with the workflow, then choose the tool.

archetypes.name: The Expansion Blind Spot

constraintPattern.description: NRR below 100%, CS team is reactive (support tickets) not proactive (growth), no expansion playbook, upsell is accidental, no QBR process

sequence: Built customer health score from 5 signals — usage, NPS, support tickets, engagement, payment (week 1-3)
Trained CS team on expansion selling — QBR template with growth conversation (week 3-6)
Introduced quarterly business reviews for top 20% of accounts by ACV (week 6-8)
Created expansion triggers — automated alerts when usage crosses tier thresholds (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: The first round of QBRs with top accounts revealed 4 accounts actively evaluating competitors — early save interventions retained €35k MRR

outcome.days90: NRR improved from 92% to 103%. 3 expansion deals closed from QBR conversations. Saved 4 at-risk accounts worth €35k MRR.
outcome.months6: NRR at 112%. CS-sourced expansion revenue became 20% of new MRR. Top-quartile accounts growing 15% annually via natural expansion.

archetypes.keyLesson: The cheapest revenue is expansion revenue — CAC is nearly zero because you've already earned trust. Yet most early-scale companies treat CSMs as support agents instead of growth agents. Shifting one QBR-trained CSM to proactive expansion typically generates more revenue than an additional AE.

archetypes.name: The ICP Fog

constraintPattern.description: No clear ICP definition, selling to anyone who will listen, messaging changes per prospect, can't articulate who the ideal customer is in one sentence

sequence: Analyzed best 5 customers: who paid fastest, churned least, referred most (week 1-2)
Built ICP profile from pattern: industry, size, pain point, trigger event (week 2-3)
Rewrote all messaging for ONE buyer persona (week 3-5)
Said no to 3 out-of-ICP inbound leads as discipline test (week 5-8)
intervention.keyAction: Saying no to paying customers who didn't fit ICP was the hardest decision — but it freed capacity for 2 ideal-fit customers who became case studies and referral sources

outcome.days90: Win rate improved from 8% to 22% on ICP-match prospects. Sales cycle shortened 35%. First customer referral from ideal-fit account.
outcome.months6: 80% of pipeline is ICP-match. Average deal size up 50% because messaging resonates with right buyers. First 3 inbound leads from referrals.

archetypes.keyLesson: ICP isn't a marketing exercise — it's a resource allocation decision. At seed stage, every hour spent on an out-of-ICP prospect is an hour stolen from an ideal-fit one. The companies that grow fastest are the ones willing to say no to revenue that doesn't compound.

archetypes.name: The Segmentation Gap

constraintPattern.description: Same sales process for €5k and €50k deals, no segmentation by company size or use case, pricing is flat, SMB and enterprise prospects get same treatment, AEs complain about deal quality

sequence: Segmented existing customer base into 3 tiers by ACV (week 1-2)
Designed distinct sales motions: self-serve (SMB), mid-touch (MM), high-touch (ENT) (week 2-6)
Built segment-specific pricing and packaging (week 6-8)
Assigned AEs to segments based on selling style and track record (week 8-12)
intervention.keyAction: Analyzing existing customers revealed the top 15% by ACV contributed 55% of revenue but received the same service as the bottom 15% — rebalancing attention immediately improved retention in the top tier

outcome.days90: Enterprise segment ACV increased 30%. SMB self-serve reduced AE time by 60%. Overall win rate improved 15%.
outcome.months6: Three distinct motions running. Enterprise NRR at 125%. Total MRR grew 40% with same headcount.

archetypes.keyLesson: When your AEs say 'deal quality is inconsistent,' they're telling you that you have a segmentation problem, not a pipeline problem. One GTM motion works until ~€100k MRR — after that, treating all customers the same penalizes your best accounts and over-invests in your worst.

archetypes.name: The Hiring Ahead Trap

constraintPattern.description: Hired 4+ salespeople but average quota attainment is <60%, burn rate accelerated but revenue didn't follow, management overhead increased, pipeline per rep is thin

sequence: Measured quota attainment and pipeline per rep honestly (week 1-2)
Identified top performers and their specific winning patterns (week 2-4)
Reduced team by 30%, invested savings in enablement for remaining reps (week 4-6)
Built ramp plan: no new hires until existing reps hit 80% quota (week 6-12)
intervention.keyAction: Honest quota attainment measurement showed 2 of 6 reps carrying 75% of revenue — the others were pipeline tourists. Reducing the team actually improved morale and focus.

outcome.days90: Team quota attainment went from 45% to 78%. Burn rate reduced 25%. Revenue per rep doubled.
outcome.months6: Remaining team at 90%+ attainment. Two new hires made with proven playbook. Burn multiple improved from 3.2x to 1.4x.

archetypes.keyLesson: Hiring is the most expensive experiment in sales. Every unproductive AE costs ~€15k/month fully loaded. The rule: don't hire your 3rd AE until your first 2 are consistently at 80%+ quota. If they're not, the playbook is the problem, not the headcount.

archetypes.name: The Post-Funding Drift

constraintPattern.description: Post-fundraise, launched 5+ initiatives simultaneously, team stretched across competing priorities, no single bet is getting enough resources to succeed, weekly priorities keep shifting

sequence: Forced-ranked all initiatives using ICE scoring (impact, confidence, effort) (week 1-2)
Killed or paused 3 of 5 initiatives — committed to top 2 for 90 days (week 2-3)
Assigned clear ownership and weekly metrics to each surviving initiative (week 3-4)
Instituted 'no new initiatives' rule for 90 days unless CEO override (week 4-12)
intervention.keyAction: The CEO's willingness to publicly kill initiatives they had championed was the cultural turning point — it gave the team permission to focus

outcome.days90: Both surviving initiatives hit 90-day milestones. Team satisfaction and clarity scores improved 40%. Pipeline contribution from focused initiative 2.5x previous quarter.
outcome.months6: One initiative became a core growth driver (+35% MRR). Failed initiatives reintroduced selectively with proper resourcing. Execution velocity doubled.

archetypes.keyLesson: Post-funding euphoria is the most dangerous period for a scaling company. More money doesn't mean more bets — it means bigger bets. The companies that deploy capital fastest are usually the ones that fail fastest. Discipline is doing fewer things with more resources, not more things with more resources.
